mission.title: Install and Configure Drasi on Kubernetes
mission.description: Drasi is a data processing platform that simplifies detecting changes in data and taking immediate action. Installing Drasi allows you to monitor system logs and change feeds for specific events, enabling real-time actionable insights without the overhead of traditional data processing methods.

steps.title: Add the Drasi Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Drasi Helm chart repository to your local Helm setup using the following command:
```bash
helm repo add drasi https://charts.drasi.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Drasi using Helm
steps.description:
Install Drasi in a new namespace called 'drasi' with the following command:
```bash
helm install drasi drasi/drasi --namespace drasi --create-namespace --version 1.0.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check the status of the Drasi pods to ensure they are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace drasi
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the Drasi deployment to set resource limits. Use the following command to open the deployment configuration:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment drasi --namespace drasi
```
Then, add the following under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for access
steps.description:
Forward a local port to the Drasi service to access it:
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/drasi --namespace drasi 8080:80
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Drasi will have the pods running without errors, and you will be able to access the Drasi service through port 8080 on your local machine. You can also verify that resource limits are applied by checking the deployment configuration.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add drasi https://charts.drasi.io
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Drasi release from the cluster using the following command to remove the deployment and all associated resources:
```bash
helm uninstall drasi --namespace drasi
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) that were created during the installation:
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep drasi | awk '{print $1}')
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace drasi
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'drasi' namespace and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace drasi
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, create a backup of the current state of the Drasi release:
```bash
helm get all drasi --namespace drasi > drasi-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Drasi release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade drasi drasi/drasi --namespace drasi --version 1.1.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the Drasi pods to ensure they are running the upgraded version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace drasi
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Drasi pods are stuck in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for more information:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace drasi
``` 
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access the Drasi service, ensure that the service is running and the port forwarding is set up correctly:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace drasi
kubectl port-forward svc/drasi --namespace drasi 8080:80
``` 
Check for any firewall rules or network policies that might be blocking access.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources
troubleshooting.description:
If you see errors related to insufficient resources, check the resource limits and the available resources in your cluster:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace drasi
kubectl top nodes
``` 
Consider adjusting the resource limits or scaling your cluster.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade command fails, check for version compatibility and existing resources:
```bash
helm history drasi --namespace drasi
helm get all drasi --namespace drasi
``` 
You may need to rollback to a previous version if the upgrade cannot be completed.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
